en.extract:
The white-winged robin (Melanodryas sigillata) is a species of bird in the Australasian robin family Petroicidae.


Taxonomy
The white-winged robin was formally described in 1890 as Poecilodryas sigillata by the English naturalist Charles Walter De Vis based on a specimen collected on Mount Victoria in southeast New Guinea. The specific epithet is from Latin meaning "adorned with little figures" from "sigilla" meaning  "image", "little figure" or "mark". The white-winged robin was formerly placed in the genus Peneothello, but based on the results of a 2011 molecular genetic study of the Australasian robins by Les Christidis and coworkers, in 2025 the genus Peneothello was merged into a more broadly defined Melanodryas.
Three subspecies are recognised:

M. s. saruwagedi (Mayr, E, 1931) – montane north-eastern New Guinea (mountains of Huon Peninsula)
M. s. quadrimaculata (van Oort, ED, 1910) – montane western New Guinea (Nassau and Snow mountains)
M. s. sigillata (De Vis, CW, 1890) – montane east-central to southeastern New Guinea


Description
Measuring 14 to 15 centimetres (5.5 to 5.9 in), the adult white-winged robin has black plumage, with largely white wings. The male and female are identical. The bill and feet are black, and the eyes are dark brown. Juveniles have a variable streaked brown plumage.


Distribution and habitat
The white-winged robin is found in the New Guinea Highlands.  Its natural habitat is subtropical or tropical moist montane forests.  It is found in the highlands of New Guinea at elevations of 2,400 to 3,900 metres (7,900 to 12,800 ft) and is replaced by the slaty robin at lower elevations.


Behaviour
Within the forest the robin is found in pairs or small troops of several birds in the understory or on the ground. It is insectivorous, but does also eat some seeds.  The somewhat bulky cup-shaped nest is constructed in a tree fork.


References

The '''white-winged robin''' ('''''Melanodryas sigillata''''') is a species of bird in the Australasian robin family [[Petroicidae]].

==Taxonomy==
The white-winged robin was [[species description|formally described]] in 1890 as ''Poecilodryas sigillata'' by the English naturalist [[Charles Walter De Vis]] based on a specimen collected on [[Mount Victoria (Papua New Guinea)|Mount Victoria]] in southeast [[New Guinea]].<ref>{{ cite book | last=De Vis | first=Charles Walter | author-link=Charles Walter De Vis | date=1890 | chapter=Appendix G. Report on birds from British New Guinea | editor-last=MacGregor | editor-first=William MacGregor | editor-link=William MacGregor| title=British New Guinea. Annual report by Her Majesty's administrator of the government, from 4th September 1888 to 30th June 1889 | location=Melbourne | publisher=Robert S Brain Government Printer | pages=58-61 [59] }}</ref><ref name=mayr>{{ cite book | editor1-last=Mayr | editor1-first=Ernst | editor1-link=Ernst Mayr | editor2-last=Cottrell | editor2-first=G. William | year=1986 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=11 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology | location=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=578 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14484279 }}</ref> The specific epithet is from [[Latin]] meaning "adorned with little figures" from "sigilla" meaning  "image", "little figure" or "mark".<ref>{{ cite web | last=Jobling | first=James A. | title=sophiae | work=The Key to Scientific Names | url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/key-to-scientific-names/search?q=sigillata | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | access-date=14 November 2025 }}</ref> The white-winged robin was formerly placed in the genus ''Peneothello'',<ref name=mayr/><ref>{{ cite book | editor1-last=Dickinson | editor1-first=E.C. | editor1-link=Edward C. Dickinson | editor2-last=Christidis | editor2-first=L. | editor2-link=Leslie Christidis | year=2014 | title=The Howard & Moore Complete Checklist of the Birds of the World | volume=2: Passerines | edition=4th | location=Eastbourne, UK | publisher=Aves Press | isbn=978-0-9568611-2-2 | page=260 }}</ref> but based on the results of a 2011 molecular genetic study of the Australasian robins by [[Leslie Christidis|Les Christidis]] and coworkers, in 2025 the genus ''Peneothello'' was merged into a more broadly defined ''[[Melanodryas]]''.<ref name=christidis>{{ cite journal | last1=Christidis | first1=L. | author1-link=Leslie Christidis | last2=Irestedt | first2=M. | last3=Rowe | first3=D. | last4=Boles | first4=W.E. | last5=Norman | first5=J.A. | year=2011 | title=Mitochondrial and nuclear DNA phylogenies reveal a complex evolutionary history in the Australasian robins (Passeriformes: Petroicidae) | journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution | volume=61 | issue=3 | pages=726–738 | doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2011.08.014 }}</ref><ref name=avilist>{{ cite web | author=AviList Core Team | date=2025 | title=AviList: The Global Avian Checklist, v2025 | doi=10.2173/avilist.v2025 | doi-access=free | url=http://www.avilist.org/checklist/v2025/ | access-date=14 November 2025 }}</ref>

Three [[subspecies]] are recognised:<ref name=avilist/>
* ''M. s. saruwagedi'' ([[Ernst Mayr|Mayr, E]], 1931) – montane north-eastern [[New Guinea]] (mountains of [[Huon Peninsula]])
* ''M. s. quadrimaculata'' ([[Eduard Daniël van Oort|van Oort, ED]], 1910) – montane western [[New Guinea]] (Nassau and Snow mountains)
* ''M. s. sigillata'' ([[Charles Walter De Vis|De Vis, CW]], 1890) – montane east-central to southeastern [[New Guinea]]

==Description==
Measuring {{convert|14|to|15|cm|in}}, the adult white-winged robin has black plumage, with largely white wings. The male and female are identical. The bill and feet are black, and the eyes are dark brown. Juveniles have a variable streaked brown plumage.<ref name=coates90>{{cite book  | last = Coates  | first = Brian J.  | year = 1990  | title = The Birds of Papua New Guinea. Volume II  | publisher = Dove Publications  | location = Queensland  | pages = 197–98  | isbn = 978-0-9590257-1-2  | oclc = 153651608  }}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==
The white-winged robin is found in the [[New Guinea Highlands]].  Its natural [[habitat]] is subtropical or tropical moist [[montane forest]]s.  It is found in the highlands of New Guinea at elevations of {{convert|2400|to|3900|m|ft}} and is replaced by the [[slaty robin]] at lower elevations.

==Behaviour==
Within the forest the robin is found in pairs or small troops of several birds in the [[understory]] or on the ground. It is insectivorous, but does also eat some seeds.<ref name=coates90/>  The somewhat bulky cup-shaped nest is constructed in a tree fork.<ref name=coates90/>
